data.title: Illinois Tool Works: Growth Acceleration As Macro Headwinds Ease, PLS Drag Fades, And Self-Help Initiatives Strengthen
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Illinois Tool Works Inc. (ITW) is poised for revenue growth acceleration as macro headwinds ease and company-specific initiatives gain traction. A reversal in the interest rate cycle should help drive demand across the company’s end markets like automotive, residential, and light commercial construction. Further, the company’s focus on Customer Back Innovation (CBI) should continue to support share gains, particularly in Automotive OEM, where ITW is outperforming global vehicle builds through higher content per vehicle and rapid growth in EV platform. The company should also benefit from less drag from Product Line Simplification (PLS) initiatives in the coming quarters. Besides organic growth, the company has a strong balance sheet to pursue bolt-on M&As.

On the margin front, operating leverage on higher volumes and improving mix from product line simplification initiatives should benefit margins. The margins should also benefit from enterprise initiatives, including sourcing optimization, productivity improvement, and 80/20 portfolio optimization. Additionally, customer-backed innovation should help the company command higher pricing. In terms of valuation, the stock is trading at a discount to its historical averages. Given the good growth prospects and lower than historical valuation, I have a buy rating on the stock.

Recent Financial Performance

I last covered ITW in November 2024. At that time, the company was posting sales decline and I was optimistic about its return to growth. While the macro environment has remained tough since then, the company has returned to growth, helped by good execution.

In Q3 2025, ITW reported a 2% year-over-year revenue growth (organic growth +1%, FX +2 percentage points, product line simplification initiatives -1 percentage point.) Segment-wise, Automotive OEMs was the strongest performer with revenue growth of 7% on a reported basis and 5% on an organic basis. Food Equipment, Welding, and Specialty Products also helped, and each reported 3% Y/Y growth. However, Test and Measurement reported flat year-over-year revenues, with organic sales declining 1% due to softer demand for capital equipment. Polymers and Fluids reported revenue decline of 2% year-over-year, with organic revenues declining 3% primarily due to tough comparisons in the Polymer business. Construction Products revenue declined 1% year-over-year, with organic revenues declining 2%.

On the margin front, Operating Margin was up 90 bps year-over-year to 27.4%. Enterprise Initiatives contributed 140 bps to Margin Expansion. Further pricing and supply chain actions contributed to margin growth and helped more than offset tariff impacts.

Outlook

Moving forward, I am optimistic about ITW's growth prospects. While ITW is executing well, the broader macro headwinds particularly in construction and some industrial markets have impacted its results. However, with the Federal Reserve reversing interest rates, I expect the company's sales to further accelerate in the coming quarters.

The Federal Reserve has started cutting rates and I am expecting further cuts with Powell's term ending in May and Trump likely to pick-up someone more amenable to rate cuts as the next chair. The company has good exposure to residential and light commercial construction which could benefit as the interest rate cycle reverses. In addition, industrial capex and equipment spending should also pick up as the interest rate cycle reverses. This should help drive recovery in the company's test and measurement business, and polymers and fluids business. Certain parts of automotive OEM business, which are exposed to legacy automakers, should also benefit.

Further, I expect the company to continue executing well and gain share through customer back innovation. Customer back innovation is expected to contribute 2.3 to 2.5 percentage points to growth in 2025 and management expects its contribution to grow to 3 percentage points annually by 2030. One of the areas where the company has done really well in terms of customer back innovation is automotive OEM. This business is outperforming the global vehicle builds by 200 to 300 basis points driven by content per vehicle gains, innovation-led product differentiation and rapid growth in EV platforms.

The company is growing its EV-related revenues, helped by increasing penetration with Chinese OEMs. EV adoption coupled with increasing content penetration should continue to help the company outperform global automotive builds moving forward. In addition, as discussed earlier, interest cycle reversal should also help drive demand in this market.

The company should also benefit from less drag from product line simplification initiatives moving forward. The company is undergoing product line simplification to exit low-margin, low-growth categories. In 2025, this is expected to be an approximately 1% drag for the company. However, as the company continues to make progress in terms of divesting less strategic product lines, the incremental drag from PLS should reduce. In the steady state, management is expecting PLS drag to be closer to 50 basis points vs. the 100 basis point impact it saw last year. So, in the coming years, I believe PLS should become less of a drag which should help improve the company's overall organic growth.

Overall, when we think about end-market recovery helped by interest rate cycle reversal, customer-backed innovation’s contribution improving and PLS drag decreasing, ITW is well positioned for good organic growth moving forward.

On the margin front, the company should benefit from operating leverage as the revenue outlook is positive. ITW has done a good job in terms of cost-cutting through its enterprise initiatives and portfolio simplification, so it should show good leverage on incremental volumes. The company's incremental margins have reached around 65% in the recent quarter vs. a long-term average of 35-40%. This should help drive disproportionate earnings growth as revenues accelerate moving forward.

The company should also continue to benefit from its cost-cutting initiatives under the company's enterprise initiatives. Management is targeting approximately 125 basis points of annual margin expansion driven by sourcing optimization, productivity improvement, and 80/20 portfolio optimization.

Another good driver is product line simplification, where the company is exiting low-margin, less-differentiated product lines. This should improve the overall margin mix.

In addition, customer-backed innovation should help the company command higher pricing, due to differentiated functionality, application-specific design, and IP protection. As CBI-driven products continue to become a larger portion of overall revenue base, it should help structural improvement in pricing.

The company has also done a good job in terms of offsetting tariff-related headwinds through pricing. Also, almost 93% of the company's production occurs locally in end markets, which gives the company insulation against tariff volatility. However, if tariffs were to increase further in the coming period, the company would be able to offset its impact through price increases and local manufacturing.

So I remain optimistic about the company's margin prospects driven by good volume leverage, ongoing cost-cutting initiatives, portfolio simplification, and innovation-driven mix improvement.

In addition to good organic growth and margin improvement prospects, the company also has a strong balance sheet with its net leverage of around 1.75x EBITDA (Net debt = $8,018 mn, TTM EBITDA =$4,593 mn). This gives the company optionality to pursue bolt-on M&As to further add to organic growth and margin improvement initiatives.

Valuation

ITW is trading at 22.77x FY26 consensus EPS estimate of $11.28 and 21.16x FY27 consensus EPS estimate of $12.13. This is a discount versus its five-year average forward P/E of 24.88x.

I see a good chance of the multiple re-rating to historical levels as the company's growth re-accelerates in FY26 and the company also continues to see good margin improvement. The company's business mix has improved over the past several years with its divesting low growth, low margin businesses through product line simplification initiative while focusing on customer-backed innovation which has improved growth and margin profile.

However, macro headwinds in certain end markets and PLS has impacted the company’s growth and prevented full impact of these initiatives to show up in the reported numbers. With macros improving helped by interest rate cuts and PLS drag moderating, result in accelerating growth and earnings. The company also has a healthy balance sheet with net leverage of ~1.75x which provides it optionality for growth through bolt-on M&As. Further, ITW stock offers a reasonable dividend yield of approximately ~2.5%. Even if we assume the stocks valuation multiple remains at the current levels, ITW’s high single-digit EPS growth over the coming years coupled with ~2.5% dividend yield should result in approximately 10% total return for investors. So, I find this reward-to-risk attractive at current levels and rate this stock a buy.

Risks

My thesis anticipates a reversal in the interest rate cycle to support recovery in the company’s end markets, including residential and light commercial construction. If interest rate cuts are delayed or smaller in magnitude than anticipated, the recovery in demand may not happen as expected. This could impact the company’s financial performance.

Another area to monitor will be the inorganic growth through acquisitions. ITW also relies on bolt-on acquisitions to complement its organic growth efforts. There are risks associated with M&As like potential overpayment for assets, integration issues, or not realizing the intended synergies.

Takeaway

ITW has good growth potential moving forward. The revenue growth should benefit from the interest rate cycle reversal, customer back innovation, decreasing product line simplification drag, and inorganic growth prospects from M&As. The margins should benefit from operating leverage, product line simplification actions, and cost-cutting initiatives. The valuation is also lower than historical. Given the good growth prospects and lower than historical valuation, I rate ITW stock a buy.

data.title: Quantum Computing: Plenty Of Cash, Still Waiting On Traction
data.description:
Over the last few months, I’ve looked at Quantum Computing Inc. (NASDAQ:QUBT) as just another quantum stock that’s just riding high on speculation and in a similar boat as BTQ Technologies, which I’ve covered before. For one, they both have tiny revenue bases. They also have very heavy operating losses, and they spend as if there’s a much bigger business to sustain all of this within the short term.

However, I think that’s where the similarities end, and what has pulled me into covering QUBT is its balance sheet and recent acquisitions. The company has raised an extraordinary amount of capital relative to where its business is today, so it is not a survival or liquidity story. For me, the real question is whether management can turn years of R&D, photonics know-how, and technical validation into a viable commercial engine. That’s a different kind of risk, and although I’m not ready to be constructive on the stock yet, I’d like to take a closer look at it today.

What QUBT Does: An Overview Of The Company

QUBT operates under one banner, but its business is really a blend of three activities that are still lumped together, at least for now.

First, it builds photonics-based optimization systems under its Dirac platform. These are not general-purpose quantum computers. They are purpose-built systems that aim to improve optimization workloads, with the pitch that they can accelerate certain classes of problems while running at room temperature.

It is also building thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) photonic chip capabilities, and it is already operating a commercial foundry in Tempe, Arizona, that serves external customers. I think this part gets a bit less attention because it doesn’t carry the same quantum buzzwords, but it is the piece that can eventually support a more repeatable business model.

Finally, QUBT earns most of its revenue today from services, which include R&D contracts, pilots, and custom engagements, often tied to government agencies and early commercial users.

I’d say that the company’s USP is its focus on photonics technology, and I’m watching how it performs while searching for a durable commercialization path.

Product Stack, Customers, And How Monetization Is Supposed To Work

Seeing as QUBT’s products and customers are basically inseparable at this stage, I treat them as one story.

On the Dirac side, the company targets optimization workloads that show up in logistics, scheduling, imaging, and security-adjacent use cases. QUBT is now offering cloud access to these systems, and this is important because it opens the door to product-oriented revenue rather than pure services. That said, we’re still at the early stage of adoption, and the evidence base is thin.

Conversely, it looks like the company’s foundry and photonic chip effort is where the business model can change meaningfully. Thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) is becoming increasingly relevant for photonics applications in AI infrastructure, datacom, and sensing, and this is a big market opportunity by any metric. QUBT’s own website says the market size for the technology could reach $2 billion by 2029, compared to today’s value of around $200 million. Other research papers I’ve seen put the figure a bit higher, although I did see one that said it could reach $11 billion by 2033. I doubt the latter because of the discrepancy between the figures, but clearly, there’s a lot of money to be made.

There are very few commercial-scale TFLN foundries today, especially outside of China, and QUBT operates the only TFLN fab that can produce 150mm wafers in the US, so that is another major strategic advantage. If QUBT can establish itself as a credible supplier, even at a modest scale, it can move from one-off pilots to repeat purchase behavior. That is what the income statement needs, and I cannot overstate how important the foundry is to QUBT’s story moving forward. Before it opened the foundry, a large part of its revenue came from services. Now, though, it can at least start to sell something repeatable. We can see some signs of this with the numbers over the last year or so.

Revenue progression (FY24 → FY25 (9M))

Period

Revenue ($000)

Commentary

FY2024

Shows direction, not scale

Q3 2025 was a standout, and that’s because it was the first quarter where the revenue reflected:

  • increased activity on custom hardware and R&D services, and

  • the beginning of revenue recognition tied to cloud-based access to Dirac systems

It is a transition, to be clear, but the crucial thing is that we’re seeing signs of progress.

Following on from that is the customer mix today, which reflects how early this is. If you go through the company’s earnings filings, you’ll see that government agencies and research institutions dominate, although there are a small number of early commercial buyers. Still, that mix comes with long cycles, uneven purchase timing, and lumpy revenue. It also explains why services still dominate. I suppose it’s a tradeoff that the company has to make, though. Those institutions are the only ones with the funds and patience to spend and wait for a developing technology like this.

However, if we just look back to last month, December 2025, it’s clear that QUBT wants to speed things up. At the time, it struck a deal to acquire Luminar Semiconductor, Inc. for $110 million in cash, and it expects to close in January 2026 pending bankruptcy court approval. In my view, that’s a sign that the company is no longer satisfied with waiting for organic commercialization to gradually arrive. It wants an operating photonics footprint, IP, and an existing top engineering team.

Then, just yesterday, January 12, 2026, it added another layer by becoming the stalking horse bidder for select remaining assets of Luminar Technologies’ LiDAR business, with an initial bid of about $22 million, subject to auction and court approval. In my opinion, this bid for LiDAR assets changes the risk profile, because it pushes QUBT into a carve-out and auction dynamic where the outcomes are not fully under its control. For example, it can still lose the assets to a higher bid, and while stalking horse protections exist, getting outbid doesn’t create any operating value.

Let us assume that all goes well, though. I believe that this is where the move becomes more than strategic. It is now a capital allocation test. Why? Well, buying distressed assets can be a smart move, especially when you consider where QUBT is in its lifecycle, but it also introduces integration risks. It is also possible that the company is just accumulating complexity faster than it accumulates revenue.

At the same time, we must view these moves within a broader context. Luminar Technology’s (LAZRQ) Chapter 11 bankruptcy process is largely forced because it lost a major contract with Volvo, and it is also facing some leadership issues. That is exactly why the assets are available and why the purchase price is not the full price. If QUBT can complete this deal in addition to the initial semiconductor acquisition, then it is essentially getting crucial assets much cheaper than it otherwise would have. So, the onus is on it to retain the talent, stabilize customers, and integrate operations with its existing business. That is easier said than done, and I’ll keep an eye on how it goes.

When I step back, the monetization path is still simple in theory. QUBT needs to move from services to products, from pilots to repeat orders, and from validation to scale. The Luminar moves can accelerate that transition. They can also expose whether management can execute under pressure.

The Numbers Force The Conversation

Once I get past the technology and vision, the numbers still pull me back to reality. The fact is that QUBT does not have a revenue problem in the abstract. It has a revenue problem relative to its ambition and cost base, and that isn’t surprising at all.

Yes, revenue exists, but it is small and uneven. One better quarter like Q3 helps, but it does not change the shape of the business by itself, especially when we still don’t have visibility into the near-term revenue potential. Also, the (gross) margin is fairly solid, albeit it is far from stable too. It’s been as high as 55% and as low as 30% over the last three full fiscal years, so we can’t draw any reasonable inferences there either.

The real anchor is operating expenses. QUBT runs a business that currently costs around $10 million per quarter to operate. I understand why R&D is heavy, but that cost base sets the rules of the game, and the company is still nowhere near covering it through operations.

However, you might also see some “profitable” quarters like Q1 and Q3 FY25, and then you think the company is doing better than it actually is. That’s not the case, and those profits have typically come from non-operating items, especially fair value changes related to its derivative liabilities and interest income on a large cash balance. They’re just accounting tricks.

The balance sheet, though, is a different case entirely. QUBT has raised so much capital that I cannot countenance any near or medium-term financial stress. The company had around $555 million in cash and short-term investments at the end of Q3, and it raised another $750 million in October, so management has a lot of room to invest and experiment. I already touched on the Luminar deals, and it is likely that they’ll be looking at more bolt-on acquisitions over the next few years.

The thing is, that level of financial strength for such an operationally small business creates a new tension. The company has all the money it needs to keep running for a very long time, but now, it needs to prove it can use capital well. The questions on every investor’s lips are probably going to be along the lines of: Am I seeing movement toward products and repeat customers, or am I watching a well-funded R&D engine keep running?

That’s why the dilution matters so much here. Shareholders have already paid for the time, and what comes next for QUBT will determine whether they’ll get real value for that time.

What I’m Looking Out For

Looking ahead, I believe that QUBT’s story is all about the proof. For one, it would be nice if revenue stopped resetting every quarter. That’s unlikely in the near term, but it’s something to expect sooner than later, given the moves and current market opportunities for the company’s existing technology and infrastructure. That includes the TFLN foundry and Luminar Semiconductor acquisition. They give QUBT a path that doesn’t fully rely on whenever the broader market decides to adopt a quantum-based or supported future timeline.

Leading on from that, I expect operational spending to increase, given integration costs and other associated expenses. The only criterion for not judging the extra spending as wasteful is if it translates into clearer commercial signals. It is one thing to have money in the bank, and it is another to use it to improve traction. Given the context of the Luminar Semiconductor deal, I expect QUBT to do a good job of integrating that operating photonics capability and using it to build credibility and utilization. That’s where the revenue and accompanying upside will come from.

Valuation: This Is Still A Story Stock

As I write this, QUBT is trading at $11.75/share, and it is up ¬80% over the last year. Conversely, the stock price is just 10% higher than it was 5 years ago, when it was just getting into the quantum computing game. As you can tell, the traditional valuation tools don’t help much here. The revenue base is too small and too unstable, and the operating losses are still structural, so we still have to go back to the balance sheet.

As I’ve mentioned before, QUBT holds an unusually large pool of cash and investments relative to the business it operates today. That private placement last October raised around $750 million, selling 37 million shares to investors. With those numbers, the price/share came out to ¬$20, which is nearly twice today’s price. Yes, that liquidity lowers the near-term downside risk and gives management years of runway, but it also comes with major dilution risks. In practical terms, investors are paying for time, optionality, and long-term execution, not any near-term revenue potential.

The company's market cap is ~$2.8 billion, and its enterprise value is ~$2.25 billion. This is a business with under a million dollars in annual revenue, and with around $2.5 billion in debt, the leverage changes how I think about the risk. Say, for example, that QUBT manages to scale to $40-50 million in annual revenue over the next five years, and then up to $100 million in another two years after that. Today's $2.25 billion EV would imply that the EV/Sales multiple for the former range drops down to 45-56x, and at $100 million, around 23x. Those are extremely inflated premiums over the sector median of around 3x EV/Sales, so clearly, even the very optimistic commercialization scenarios are already heavily baked in at this price.

This is exactly why the stock sits in a valuation gray zone for me. If the foundry begins to attract repeat demand and product revenue compounds, then I think the valuation can grow into itself. Conversely, I think it’s reasonable to assume that the market will eventually stop paying for possibility and start anchoring to what the business earns if we don’t see more positive development around commercialization and revenue.

So I treat valuation here as a question of confidence. I’m not comfortable underwriting a Buy just yet, at least not without clearer traction. At the same time, I can’t call this a Sell because the floor for the stock is pretty high, given the balance sheet and the strategic leverage management has created for itself.

Risks And Catalysts

I'd say the biggest risk to QUBT at this point, is time. There's a lot of leverage on the balance sheet, so the company doesn't have the luxury of open-ended experimentation. The market is already pricing in a future where the photonics and foundry strategy works. So, it cannot afford to any drags in commercialization. If that happens, we will see the valuation compress really quickly.

Leading on from that is capital allocation. I've discussed the Luminar Semiconductor and asset acquisitions, and we need to see a steady cadence with integration. Any delays with that or translating these assets into revenue matters more now. With that debt on the balance sheet, it is crucial that any move the company makes helps it start making money.

Product-led revenue needs to scale, not just one-off services, and ideally, we'd start seeing some moves from the Luminar integration. I'm assuming that it starts recognizing decent revenue by 2026 or 2027, and that revenue needs to stay consistent from quarter-to-quarter. If that happens, and we see some growth on a bi-annual basis, then that would change how the market frames the business. With the foundry, if external customers begin to place recurring orders, then it is easier for me to defend the optionality in today's price.

I’m At A Hold

Financially speaking, QUBT is in a great place. What needs to happen next is clear progress on an execution front, across all of its business lines and acquisitions. I can see the appeal behind the stock, especially because it is a quantum computing mover, but I’d rather watch the story closely for a bit longer before dipping my toes into the water.

data.title: Gildan Activewear: Identifying Post-M&A Catalysts
data.description:
I've maintained a "Buy" rating for Gildan Activewear Inc. (GIL) (GIL:CA).

GIL's better-than-expected 3Q25 financials and its planned purchase of a 100% stake in HanesBrands/HBI were detailed in my previous Oct. 30, 2025, update.

The transaction was done last month. There are a number of post-M&A re-rating triggers for the firm, which I will outline in the subsequent sections. That explains why I still view this stock as an appealing investment candidate.

A Beneficiary Of Fund Inflows

The delisted HBI was taken out from "the S&P SmallCap 600" according to a Nov. 25, 2025, SA News piece. My take is that changes to other indices will have a good impact on its acquirer.

The "S&P/TSX Composite Index" was last rebalanced in Dec '25. GIL's weight in the former was raised by 6bps (basis points) to 0.30% based on Capital IQ data. That happened because its market capitalization got bigger with the issuance of new shares to finance HBI's investment.

The index-driven buying of the company's stock by passive investors would have materialized earlier.

Going forward, I think certain benchmark-hugging active managers may also add to their GIL positions to minimize tracking errors.

ASEAN-Focused Rivals Might Cede Ground To Gildan

The HBI acquisition adds to GIL's existing Central American production capacity. This gives it a chance to take market share away from some competitors dependent on sourcing from ASEAN.

Unifi (UFI) spoke about the reasonably modest "10% (U.S.) tariff for Central America" at the 1QFY26 results briefing. It also stressed that "there is an opportunity for some of the brands to claim back some of that 10% reciprocal tariff" for "CAFTA-DR (Dominican Republic–Central America Free Trade Agreement) goods."

Excluding Singapore (10%), the U.S.'s levies imposed on "Association of Southeast Asian Nations/ASEAN" are relatively more onerous, in the 19%-40% range.

Asset Monetization Could Generate Meaningful Proceeds

My late-October 2025 write-up highlighted GIL's "review of strategic alternatives" that may "involve the disposal of HBI's sub-scale, low-margin international operations" mainly "located in Oceania."

HanesBrands' Australian division sells ladies' innerwear, which its parent has no expertise in. It's also heavily reliant on third-party manufacturing partners, unlike the vertically integrated GIL. These represent reasons to consider a divestiture.

The non-U.S. turnover derived by HBI in the recent full-year period (FY24) was around $1.1 billion.

As per Capital IQ, the median consensus forward "Price-to-Sales" for major Australia-listed retailers is 0.64 times. I've chosen Accent (AX1.AX), Baby Bunting (BBN.AX), and Super Retail Group (SSRG.F)(SUL.AX) for this peer benchmarking exercise.

My estimates suggest that HBI's overseas businesses might be worth $700 million (0.64x1,100). That's equivalent to about 7% of GIL's market cap.

Anticipating An Improvement In Capital Management

The group's "Net Debt-to-EBITDA/Leverage" metric is projected to rise by +0.6 turns to 2.6x following the HBI deal. It also paused repurchases in tandem with the M&A announcement.

My opinion is that GIL's outlook pertaining to balance sheet strength and cash distributions will be better in the future.

The sell-side's forecasts point to the firm's net leverage declining to 2.13x and 1.72x (source: S&P Capital IQ) by end-FY26 and end-FY27, respectively. I agree with the analysts' view regarding a positive change in its future credit profile.

In the preceding section, I touched on possible cashflow derived from asset sales. That can utilized for debt pay-down.

Also, my Oct. 30, 2025, article wrote about GIL's targeted "300bps of EBITDA margin accretion by exploiting synergies with the acquiree (HBI)" through initiatives like lifting "its production utilization." This could lead to higher earnings/cash flows available for deleveraging.

Separately, the company's expanded financial capacity resulting from deal synergy and divestments can also support a restart of buybacks.

GIL indicated at its 3Q2025 call that it only "assumed no share repurchases for the remainder of 2025, as indicated at the time of the announcement of the proposed HanesBrands acquisition."

The stock's forward buyback yield may be as high as 8%, assuming that it spends the same amount ($0.76 billion) as that for '24.

If the share purchases drop to the 2021-2024 average of $0.45B, the metric is still a pretty decent 4%.

You Need To Contemplate These Bear-Case Scenarios

GIL might fail to find a suitable buyer for HBI's international operations. That will limit its ability to deleverage and return capital to stockholders.

Unfavorable index adjustments may translate into outflows for the firm's shares.

My Concluding Thoughts

I'm staying bullish on this name.

GIL is now trading at a 14x FY2026 P/E. The mean earnings ratio for apparel businesses V.F. Corporation (VFC) and Deckers Outdoor (DECK) is 20.2 times.

The stock's upside is +44% (20.2/14) benchmarked with VFC/DECK, which warrants a "Buy." In this update, I've identified many catalysts that should allow it to command a richer valuation multiple.

data.title: Trump Puts Silver, Other Critical Minerals Tariffs On Hold
data.description:
By Ewa Manthey, Commodities Strategist

Silver prices briefly tumbled from record highs after US President Donald Trump said he is holding off on new tariffs targeting imports of critical minerals, easing near-term trade disruption risks

Silver fell more than 7% on Thursday, retreating from an all-time high above $93/oz, as broad tariff threats receded. However, prices have since recovered much of the earlier decline, signalling that traders are repricing the market’s structural drivers. The recent recovery underscores silver's well-known volatility, which is typically higher than gold due to its smaller market size and dual role as both industrial and investment metal.

The decision reduced part of the near-term policy risk premium that had propelled silver to record levels. Despite the correction, silver prices remain more than 25% higher year-to-date, highlighting the strength of underlying market dynamics.

Trump said the administration would instead pursue bilateral agreements with key trading partners to secure adequate supplies of critical minerals and consider a price floor on imports, with officials expected to report back within 180 days. While tariffs were not ruled out entirely, the shift in tone reduced the immediate disruption risk.

The decision follows a months-long Section 232 review under the Trade Expansion Act, assessing whether imports of processed critical minerals threaten US national security.

The tariff review covered a broad set of processed critical minerals beyond silver, from lithium, cobalt, nickel, rare earths and gallium to graphite, platinum group metals and other industrial metals, along with any goods incorporating them such as semi-finished goods and final components used in electric vehicles, batteries, permanent magnets, and electronics.

Silver flows remain elevated in the US

US tariff uncertainty had already pulled significant volumes of physical silver from London to the US, triggering a historic squeeze. This led to a sharp decline in available silver stocks in London, the primary trading hub, amplifying price moves and volatility.

Silver has outpaced gold sharply, with prices up nearly 150% over the past year, supported by both safe-haven flows and robust industrial demand. This has pushed the gold/silver ratio to just above 50, the lowest level since 2011.

Gold/silver ratio drops to lowest since 2011

Beyond policy-driven volatility, silver continues to face a structural supply deficit. Mine supply growth remains constrained, as most of the silver production is a by-product of other metals, limiting the industry’s ability to respond quickly to higher prices. At the same time, industrial demand linked to solar, electrification, and electronics remains strong, keeping the physical market tight.

This persistent deficit underpins silver’s bullish case and helps explain why prices have remained elevated despite fluctuations in the speculative risk premium.

Volatility is likely to remain elevated as silver’s dual role as both an industrial and investment metal continues to drive larger percentage swings than gold, especially given its smaller overall market size.

In the near term, prices may consolidate in a range as tariff risks are reassessed and positioning normalises. However, structural deficits, tight physical availability, and ongoing policy uncertainty suggest downside might be limited, with silver likely to remain well-supported on dips. Trade policy developments, macro conditions, and any renewed signs of supply disruptions will remain key catalysts.

Silver’s role in industrial demand and constrained physical supply support a positive structural backdrop, but risks tied to trade policy and macro sentiment will remain key drivers of price dynamics.

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: TG Therapeutics: The "Exit Velocity" You Can't Ignore (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
My last article on TG Therapeutics (TGTX) followed its first quarter 2025 earnings report, in which BRIUMVI contributed nearly $120 million, which represented a 130% increase year-over-year. As I've expressed in the past, however, TG was pretty slow to develop a subcutaneous version of BRIUMVI, which would effectively extend its total addressable market. Instead, subcutaneous BRIUMVI will theoretically enter the market no earlier than a few years after rivals like Novartis' (NVS) KESIMPTA and Roche's (RHHBY) OCREVUS ZUNOVO. Given a mixed bag of BRIUMVI outperformance, a hefty $5.5 billion valuation, and MS market dynamics, I recommended a Hold.

TG has since reported preliminary Q4 results and guided for 2026. After careful review of FY25 performance, pipeline cadence, and BRIUMVI’s outlook, I have never been as bullish on TGTX as I am today.

Recent Developments

To say the least, BRIUMVI has outperformed in the MS market. If you look at the quarterly ramp in 2025, it's impressive. After achieving around $120 million in the first quarter, BRIUMVI went on to generate around $139 million, $153 million, and $182 million in the second, third, and fourth quarters, respectively. What's most impressive is that if you look at the sequence, it actually implies accelerating exit velocity into the year-end.

After looking at the company's 2026 guidance, it's clear that BRIUMVI's revenue ramp has not closed. TG expects 2026 BRIUMVI U.S. net revenue of between $825 million and $850 million. After doing some napkin math, this implies roughly 40% growth versus full-year 2025 U.S. net revenue. And if you annualize the fourth quarter figure, the midpoint of the 2026 U.S. guidance is roughly 15% above the annualized fourth quarter run rate. So its guidance is assuming continued growth beyond the fourth quarter exit

TG also broke down operating expenses. It expects its core operating expenses, or R&D and SG&A, to fall around $350 million in 2026. Then there are one-time expenses tied to the subcutaneous inventory build-out of around $100 million. In any event, this still implies that the company will remain comfortably profitable this year.

So what is going on? How is it that TG is doing so well in this competitive market?

As implied by its 2026 guidance being above the annualized fourth quarter run rate, the company is expecting continued base expansion, rather than merely holding on to its "slice of the pie."

TG laid out its strategy during the J.P. Morgan conference call.

First and foremost, they have strategically priced BRIUMVI at a low price point. So there is a clear focus on access first, which is a bit different from other anti-CD20 therapies in the MS market and eases payer pushback. This is helping the company capture a lot of volume, and given the high prices of these therapies, it's not having to sacrifice too much on margins.

The company also continues to make efforts to decrease administrative friction with BRIUMVI. It expects top-line ENHANCE data in mid-2026. This aims at simplifying onboarding by consolidating the Day 1 and Day 15 initiation doses into a single start dose, with maintenance continuing every 24 weeks.

Regarding its subcutaneous trial, which is TG's main TAM expansion lever, TG is targeting top-line data at the end of 2026 or early 2027. Management described the pivotal approach as PK-matching (to intravenous BRIUMVI) over a 24-week period.

It's clear that TG is optimizing for launch execution and supply continuity instead of just waiting for perfect certainty from the pivotal readout. This also means that reported margins/cash flow are going to look noisier this year in the build-out period, even as the underlying commercial engine is strengthening.

The company is also exploring BRIUMVI in indications outside of MS. And its allogenic CD19 CAR-T, azer-cel, is still there as well, with data expected in the second half of 2026, and plans to explore additional autoimmune indications like myasthenia gravis. I would treat these prospects as long-dated call options and keep the focus on IV and subcutaneous BRIUMVI in MS.

Valuation Assumptions

The stock is roughly 15% cheaper than my last look, which inspired me to conduct a discounted cash flow analysis.

As for model assumptions, "Year 1 Revenue" of $850 million represents TG's 2026 estimates. I modeled for 20% revenue growth and 20% free cash flow margins over the 9-year projection. Depending on time and source, stock beta differs, but I went with 1.35.

Most everything else was either gathered from financial material or is standard discounted cash flow analysis methodology.

Now, as I sometimes caution, that 20% free cash flow margin estimate is an "assumption with an assumption" because TG is not yet generating "cash from operations." So that is really an expectation that the company will grow into that figure on the wings of its subcutaneous offering, which figures to have much better margins.

Some of the growth could be achieved by intravenous BRIUMVI alone because the global MS therapeutics market is expected to grow around 6% per year. In other words, even if BRIUMVI simply keeps its market share the same, it could still experience growth. It is more likely that BRIUMVI continues to gain market share, as evidenced by its recent performance. Moreover, a subcutaneous at-home BRIUMVI would further expand its total addressable market, which figures to validate my 15% CAGR estimate.

That said, this should really be treated as a quick “sanity check.” My model is purposefully simplistic and does not account for all of the complexities associated with, what is essentially, a single franchise pharma company. Still, it's important to at least get a general idea of what a stock's valuation implies about expectations.

Because my discounted cash flow model inherently presents only one outcome, I also performed a break-even analysis.

The stock's valuation could support anywhere between roughly 13% to 21% CAGR and 16% to 24% free cash flow margin over the projection period, which may read conservative given BRIUMVI's performance and expansion opportunities.

Final Words

TGTX is nearly 15% cheaper since my last look, and TG's prospects have materially improved. This merits a rating upgrade from Hold to Strong Buy. BRIUMVI is clearly on pace to become a blockbuster drug, which is something I admittedly have expressed doubts about in the past. So kudos to management on its ability to capture market share in a market where several other options exist.

Although TG was perhaps understandably slow (limited capital) to pivot to the subcutaneous market, this is becoming an immediate prospect for the company, and I suspect that the market will slowly begin to price in this expansion opportunity accordingly.

Risks to my thesis include competition within the MS treatment market, sustained R&D expenditure without proportionate ROI, clinical trial failures, regulatory setbacks, and MS marketplace dynamics that hinder BRIUMVI’s pricing power and/or uptake.

data.title: Biogen: Why I Anticipate A Difficult 2026 For 'End Of An Era' Pharma
data.description:
Investment Overview

When I last covered Biogen Inc. (BIIB), the Cambridge, Massachusetts based pharma and central nervous system ("CNS") disease specialist, in a note for Seeking Alpha last February, I awarded its stock my first ever Strong Sell rating.

I noted that:

For the full-year 2024, Biogen revenues amounted to $9.7bn, down 2% year-on-year, while GAAP diluted EPS was up 40%, to $11.21, and non-GAAP EPS $16.47, up 12% year-on-year.

In terms of guidance for 2025, Biogen forecast for an EPS of $15.25-$16.25, with total revenues expected to decline by a mid-single digit percentage at constant currency, which management puts down to declining revenues within its Multiple Sclerosis franchise, "partially offset by revenue growth from newly launched products."

Multiple Sclerosis ("MS") had been Biogen's main source of revenues for many years, however I noted that franchise revenues had declined from $8.5bn in 2019, to $4.4 billion in 2024, owing to patent expirations.

I further noted that the launch of Alzheimer's drug Leqembi, in partnership with Japanese partner Eisai (ESALF), had underwhelmed, with Biogen reporting just $60m of collaboration revenues in 2024, and that there were no product approval highlights to look forward to in 2025, and potentially none in 2026 or 2027, either.

My conclusion was that "I can no longer find a single reason to get excited about Biogen stock - Sell!" At the time, Biogen stock traded at ~$138 per share, and by mid-April, it had sunk to <$115 per share, its lowest value in more than a decade, initially justifying my call.

Stock began to pick up some momentum after that, however, rising to $160 per share in October, and to ~$188 per share last week, before correcting downward to $169 per share at the time of writing.

In this update, I'll investigate why stocks picked up momentum in the second half of 2025, speculate about what 2026 may have in store for the pharma, and, based on this, advise if my Strong Sell call from last year warrants a rethink.

Biogen - Performance in 2025 To The End Of Q3

Biogen shared its Q3 earnings and business updates at the end of October last year. Total revenues amounted to $2.54bn, up 3% year-on-year, while GAAP diluted earnings per share ("EPS") increased 19% year-on-year, to $3.17. Full-year 2025 non-GAAP diluted EPS is expected to be between $14.50 and $15.00, the company says, a downgrade on initial guidance.

If we look at year-to-date revenues to the end of Q3 2025, we can see that MS franchise revenues declined slightly, from $3.28bn, to $3.12bn, with falling revenues of patent-expired Tecfidera, Plegridy, and Tysabri largely offset by growth for more recently launched (in 2019) Vumerity.

Rare disease franchise revenues showed growth, increasing from $1.45bn, to $1.64bn, largely due to growing sales of Skyclarys, indicated to treat Friedreich's Ataxia.

The Biosimilars franchise revenues declined, to $559m, while Zurzuvae, indicated for postpartum depression (having been denied approval in the much larger indication of major depressive disorder), achieved $129m of revenues - up ~160% year-on-year.

In relation to collaboration revenues, royalties from MS drug Ocrevus increased slightly, to >$1bn, as did CD20 franchise revenues, and Leqembi collaboration revenues were up nearly 300%, albeit to just $130.6m. With contract manufacturing revenues also increasing, total revenues for the period increased from $7.22bn, to $7.61bn, up ~5.4%, perhaps justifying the rise in the share price since April.

Net income for the period also increased year-on-year, to $1.95bn, from $1.9bn in the prior year period, and EPS rose from $13.02, to $13.3. The company reported $3.86bn of cash and cash equivalents, total assets of $29.2bn, and $11bn of total liabilities, including $6.3bn notes payable.

In summary, then, performance in 2025 to the end of Q3 appears reasonably solid. Growth may have been solid rather than spectacular, but at least the company's revenues and profits increased as opposed to declining.

Biogen Today - JPMorgan Conference Presentation Fails To Inspire

It may not be a coincidence that on 13th January, the day after Biogen presented at the flagship JPMorgan Healthcare conference, its share price fell from ~$178 per share, to ~$167 per share - down ~6%.

Biogen was able to report that it had achieved $1bn gross, and $800m net of savings via its "Fit for Growth" initiative, which involved reducing its workforce by ~15%. It also reported that its pipeline includes 10 Phase 3 study stage programs, with 5 potential new products, and that new product launches offset MS franchise declines in 2025 to the end of Q3.

During his presentation, Biogen's CEO and President Christopher Viehbacher, appointed in 2022, noted that:

Obviously, the MS portfolio has been declining. I think the first year I was here as CEO of Biogen, we talked about the melting iceberg. And this is a period where every pharma company gets at one point, where the growth drivers of your business are arriving at the end of their intellectual property lives and the new company has to emerge.

In November this year, Sandoz launched a generic version of Tysabri in the U.S., some two years later than originally expected, which may significantly impact that drug's revenues this year and going forward - the drug earned ~$1.3bn of revenues in 2025 to the end of Q3.

With Vumerity patents expiring around 2033, it seems clear that Biogen cannot rely on its MS franchise being its main breadwinner for much longer.

As we can see above, the current growth drivers are Leqembi, Zurzuvae, Spinraza, Skyclarys, Qalsody, and Vumerity, but each one is flawed as a major revenue driver, in my view.

Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis ("ALS") drug Qalsody will likely earn <$100m in 2025, and does not address a large enough market to grow revenues much higher, as it can only treat patients with a mutation in the SOD1 gene - analysts have set peak revenue targets at ~$300m.

Zurzuvae, with its approval restricted to post partum depression, faces a similar issue, while for all the hype around Alzheimer's drug Leqembi, which Eisai once suggested could earn ~$7bn revenues per annum, there must be serious doubts around its ability to achieve "blockbuster" revenues.

Eli Lilly's (LLY) Alzheimer's drug Kisunla, which has a similar mechanism of action ("MoA"), is performing no better than Leqembi commercially, so it seems patients and physicians lack the appetite for a drug class with limited efficacy, and concerns around safety.

Spinraza revenues will likely be lower in 2025 than they were in 2020, so there is not much reason to believe this product can be a long-term growth driver, Vumerity's patents will expire early in the next decade, and it has yet to achieve blockbuster status, while Skyclarys revenues, although growing, may also struggle to break the $1bn barrier long-term.

Attention then turns to the pipeline products, which the CEO has formerly suggested may have peak revenue potential of $9bn - $14bn. Litifilimab is expected to read out data in systemic lupus erythematosus ("SLE") this year, and could be a potential blockbuster, however competition in this market is intense, and Biogen's recent record of converting pipeline promise into tangible revenues has arguably been poor.

Likewise, Felzartamab has a Phase 3 IgAN study ongoing, alongside studies in antimicrobial resistance ("AMR") and in mitral valve insufficiency ("MVI"). I suspect the former indication is the most potentially lucrative, but there are three drugs already approved in this indication, with ten more candidates in late-stage studies, apparently.

Salanersen is a potential Spinraza successor, in spinal muscular atrophy, but would presumably cannibalise sales of Spinraza, if approved, and Dapirolizumab pegol is another SLE drug, and potentially, a competitor against litifilimab.

Looking Ahead - Am I Optimistic On Biogen's Prospects To Grow Share Price Value In 2026?

Frankly, I am not much more optimistic today than I was in February last year. Since 2020, Biogen has primarily been dependent for share price value on two things - it's MS franchise, a valuable source of multi-billion dollar revenues, and the potential of its Alzheimer's programs.

While Leqembi has been less of a disaster than its predecessor Aduhelm was (that drug has been withdrawn from the market on efficacy / safety grounds), signs that the drug can fulfil its prior peak revenue expectations are few and far between (although we may note a potential approval for a subcutaneous version in 2026), while Biogen freely admits its era of MS dominance is coming to an end.

So far as I am aware, Biogen has not provided any guidance for 2026, however I suspect revenues may decline year-on-year, primarily due to generic competition for Tysabri, and it therefore follows that EPS may also decline.

The market rarely rewards companies for shrinking revenues, so I would not be surprised to see Biogen's share price decline when Q4 earnings are announced and initial guidance provided towards the end of this month, and I see few highlights on the horizon capable of lifting the share price, or the top-line revenue figure, although an approval for SC Leqembi, and positive Litifilimab data could offset further losses.

All things considered, then, I am "upgrading" my strong Sell to a Sell rating. Biogen's current market cap of $24.84bn means price to sales ratio based on my estimated 2025 revenues of ~$10.9bn is ~2.3x, which is low, while price to earnings ("P/E") ratio would be in the region of ~11.5x based on guidance, also competitive.

Pharma valuations are complex, however, and based on future growth potential, pipeline promise, funds available to invest in M&A, track record of management, as opposed to current metrics, and in my view, Biogen scores poorly in each of these categories, having failed to deliver any new assets with bona fide Blockbuster potential, with an uninspiring pipeline, and with a well-compensated management team that has mainly focused on downsizing the business.

In summary, I'd be somewhat surprised if Biogen ends 2026 with its shares valued higher than they are today.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Italian Industrial Production Rebounds In November
data.description:
By Paolo Pizzoli, Senior Economist, Italy, Greece

The see-saw path of Italian industrial production continued in November, hinting at a very gradual upward trend. According to Istat data, seasonally adjusted industrial production increased 1.5% on the month (from -1% in October) and calendar adjusted production was up 1.4% on the year (from -0.2% in October), both stronger than expected.

All big industry groupings – energy, consumer, intermediate and instrumental goods – posted positive monthly gains, except consumer durable goods. While sector changes were modest, even the weakest performers showed signs of improvement. Year-to-date up to November, pharmaceutical and electronics production lead with roughly 2.5% yearly growth, followed by energy production, mining and quarrying and food and tobacco. At the opposite end, transport equipment and textile and apparel were the laggards, contracting by 5.6% on the year. However, this marks progress compared to early 2025, when both sectors were contracting at a double-digit yearly pace.

In general, average manufacturing production over the first 11 months of 2025 was still down 0.7% on the year.

The good news in today’s production report is that evidence of a gradual turnaround is getting stronger, signalling that the long-lasting manufacturing recession is nearing an end. In the September-November quarter, industrial production expanded by 1.1% on the previous quarter. Even stable production in December would bring a quarterly increase of 1% in the last quarter of 2025, a result unseen since 2022. This is good news for the supply side of the Italian economy. A positive contribution of industry to value-added growth in the last quarter of 2025 looks now more likely.

Looking ahead into the first quarter of 2026 is not all that clear. Geopolitical uncertainty remains elevated, US tariff developments continue, and the German pull coming from fiscal expansion might not yet be there.

Still, recent business surveys show a gradual pick-up in orders and a slow softening in inventories, good ingredients for a cyclical rebound. The tentative upward trend in the expected production sub-index looks consistent with this interpretation.

All in all, today’s production data release fits with our base case profile for GDP growth, which points to a slight acceleration in quarterly growth to c.0.2% in the fourth quarter. If realised, this would leave average annual GDP growth for 2025 at 0.6%.

data.title: Eurozone Industrial Production On The Rise
data.description:
By Bert Colijn, Chief Economist, Netherlands

Industrial production experienced a wild ride in 2025. First, it saw a significant boost from the lacklustre levels seen in late 2024 due to American frontloading of European products. After that, production petered out somewhat as American frontloading ended, but now it looks like there’s new life to the eurozone industrial recovery.

With increases in September, October and November, production now stands at the highest level in two-and-a-half years, not counting the bumper month of March when American frontloading peaked.

Getting a good sense of direction for production remains tough in the short run when it comes to industry. With more domestic investment expected, production should increase, but the question is whether we are already seeing a meaningful liftoff. The growth in November was mainly driven by capital goods production, which is now 3.6% higher than a year ago. Most large eurozone economies have seen growth in recent months.

At the same time, the manufacturing PMI has shown weakness, moving from 50.7 in August to 48.8 in December on a steady declining trend. This indicates that while optimism about the coming year remains among manufacturers, the question is whether we’ll see a continued rebound in production in the months ahead.

It may be a volatile ride, but the eurozone industry does seem to be showing more signs of life as investment plans are translated into action.

data.title: Longleaf Partners Global Fund 2025 Annual Commentary
data.description:
We had a solid year in the Global Fund marked by Southeastern's 50th anniversary and the 25th anniversary of our Global strategy. The portfolio had a mix of both larger winners and limited losers, leading to a year that beat our absolute return hurdle of inflation plus 10%. While we trailed the index, the vast majority of our underperformance came from an underweight in Financials and choosing to hold cash, along with a lack of late-in-the-cycle Industrials (especially European defense companies getting a one-time bump earlier this year). We can live with that at this point in the market, as certain parts of the "value index" are now fully priced even if they might be at lower earnings per share (EPS) multiples than the overpriced broader market.

Reflecting on Southeastern’s strengths over the past five decades led to the decision to merge our International Fund into the Global Fund, which we were pleased to complete before the end of the year. This was a large vote with our own money on the Global strategy. It is important to point out the slightly elevated number of holdings we had at year end. We are not on our way to a 25-30+ stock portfolio, rather we thought it would be better for multiple reasons to trim certain lower weights after the start of the year. There are also some related holdings such as IAC/MGM Resorts, Rayonier/PotlatchDeltic and Exor/Philips. All to say that we continue to strive for the right 20-ish stock portfolio even if the year-end number was higher.

In our last letter, we wrote about the concerning rise in circular financing at AI companies, which provides us encouragement that we are near the end of this market cycle. It is also worth discussing a broader circular theme that could additionally impact the economy when things change. There has been a uniquely S&P 500-driven wealth effect going on as highlighted in this Economist article. We have gleaned from talks with both large, sophisticated investors and smaller retail investors that there is a meaningful segment of the market with a low tax basis in stocks that feel safe or maybe just feel like they cannot be sold to book a gain. In many cases, these shares will likely not be sold until it is too late. When that happens, it usually leads to an overshoot to the downside, as we saw at formerly “safe” (and previously owned by Longleaf at lower prices) stocks like UnitedHealth and Fiserv this year. We much prefer our carefully selected portfolio of stocks that might have more quarter-to-quarter volatility but therefore have lower bars to step over from here.

Since it has now been three years since we first wrote about the “3 Rules” process improvements we made in 2022, it is time for an update. A key lesson is how much these rules help us when going into a new name and, therefore, how much better positioned we are now regarding subsequent buy or sale decisions on that name. Along these lines, the leverage rule remains the most important. Adherence to this rule has protected us from leverage pitfalls, but it should have been in place earlier so that we could better navigate situations on some of the more economically sensitive and leveraged stocks that delivered positive returns this year for the overall market, such as General Electric and Warner Bros. Discovery. The holding company rule also remains helpful for our process, and we do not have anything new to add on it.

On the other hand, it is unlikely we would have gotten all three rules exactly right three years ago, so we do have a change to the overweight rule. We are moving our limit from the initial 6.5% to 8% for a few reasons:

  • We have more data on the current research team’s historical picks and do not see material differences in performance between 6% weights and 8% weights. Furthermore, there have not been any new materially detrimental overweights in the last three years with the current team.
  • We have had investment success with our unique style of engagement for decades and hope to be able to speak more on this as 2026 progresses; this change gives us more engagement heft going forward.
  • An 8% max will also help on tax efficiency, with fewer trims of performing stocks that are growing their value per share and getting recognized for it.

Southeastern performed well in 2000 (and 1987, but our data is not as clear back then), but less so in other downturn years. We will end this part of the letter with some important points about our portfolios now that make this moment more like 1999:

First, while we did not have a separate Global strategy yet in 1999, it was a time in the market much like today when we were able to find better-priced, defensive growers like consumer staples and timberland companies. In fact, our current weighting in sectors like this combined with cash is approximately 45%, which is well above 2007 and 2021, when it was approximately 15% and we therefore had portfolios that were too risky at a time of elevated market valuations.

Second, we are confident that our individual investments are much less leveraged now than they were in 2007 and 2021. Since 2022, we have been tracking the bond and debt yields of our portfolios on a weighted average basis. We are able to apply the same methodology to our portfolios from 2007 and 2021 (data before then is more limited). The difference is material, as spreads to the US 10-year treasury for our holdings today are roughly half the levels they were in 2007 and 2021. This is a strong sign that the bond market, which can often be better than the stock market at detecting early downside risk, validates the resilience of our portfolio today.

Third, and hardest to quantify, is the high quality of management partners at our holdings. We will detail this confidence as we now turn to our notable contributors and detractors.

Notable Contributors & Detractors

Canal+ – French pay-TV operator Canal+ was a contributor for the year. The company continued to make strong operational and strategic progress across both its legacy footprint and its newly enlarged African platform after the closure of the MultiChoice merger. In Europe, the renewal of exclusive UEFA Champions League rights for the 2027–2031 cycle at economics materially better than feared reinforces the quality and durability of the company’s core subscriber base and the discipline of Canal+’s management team. At the same time, the company achieved a further positive outcome just before year end in its tax discussions with the French authorities, removing an overhang and further enhancing visibility on future FCF. Despite FCF heading in the right direction and substantial value creation to come, the share price still fails to reflect the underlying progress partly due to the fact that Canal+ remains an underfollowed French media company newly listed on the London Stock exchange.

Glanbia (GLAPF)(GLAPY) – Irish nutrition, ingredients and dairy company Glanbia contributed for the year. After a tough start in 2025, momentum in its core Performance Nutrition business improved from low single-digit growth rates. Management also delivered tangible progress on portfolio rationalization and capital allocation. The company added to its ongoing buyback program by opportunistically repurchasing an additional approximately 3% of its own shares at a discount in connection with Tirlán’s October 2025 placement, helping reduce the long-standing overhang from its largest shareholder (today an 18% holder, down from 24% earlier this year and higher levels before). At the same time, Glanbia’s management team advanced its simplification agenda by executing on the announced sale of margin-dilutive SlimFast and Body&Fit assets, sharpening focus on the higher-quality Performance Nutrition branded franchises. We have been engaged with the company and believe that additional strategic steps can be taken in the near term.

Millicom (MLCMF)(TIGO) – Latin American telecommunications operator Millicom was a contributor for the year. The company extended its strong 2024 performance through 2025, with the share price tracking operational delivery and the visible inflection in equity FCF we flagged in prior letters. This was driven by a focused new management team and an aligned majority shareholder partner in Iliad Group (Atlas). The business exceeded its already twice-raised 2024 FCF guidance and guided to material further growth in 2025, while reaching its 2.5x leverage target and increasing dividends and buybacks. The discount to our appraisal narrowed with this positive performance and solid Colombian M&A news. What was an attractive outlier at a double-digit dividend yield earlier in the year has now aligned with peers at mid-single-digit levels. With the value gap largely closed and limited upside remaining on a risk-adjusted basis, portfolio discipline dictated our exit in the third quarter.

IAC (IAC), MGM Resorts (MGM) and Angi (ANGI) – The related group of IAC, MGM Resorts and Angi contributed strongly in aggregate this year. Digital holding company IAC has signaled its plans to dispose of all assets besides People Inc. and an approximate 25% stake in MGM. Chairman Barry Diller is focused on closing the valuation disconnect as no value has been ascribed by the markets to IAC’s assets beyond MGM for too long. Share repurchase and increasing the stake in MGM are the main capital allocation options, and the company is already acting on both. One specific simplification move IAC made this year was the spinoff of Angi, the home services marketplace. We purchased more Angi shares post-spin at a depressed price and then sold our position as evidence of their multi-year turnaround heading down the right path caused the stock to reach our appraisal. Casino operator MGM Resorts had a relatively weaker 2025 in Las Vegas due to difficult comparisons after multiple years of strength. A significant turnaround at BetMGM plus strong performances at non-Las Vegas “regional” properties and Macau helped steady the consolidated business throughout the year. The general market narrative for most of the year has been that Las Vegas has peaked for various reasons. We, and IAC, believe Vegas cannot be replicated anywhere in the world, and MGM has a great position as the market leader. Management has corrected some pricing mistakes while making moves to narrow their focus. A recent sale of one of their lower-quality properties for a higher multiple than where the consolidated business trades, as well as withdrawing their New York City casino bid, freed up more capital for a management team who has repurchased over 40% of their shares at extremely attractive prices over the past five years.

Bio-Rad (BIO) and Avantor (AVTR) – Life-sciences companies Bio-Rad and Avantor combined to detract from our performance this year. We entered the year owning only Bio-Rad. The company got off to a rough start with a disappointing set of results compounded by large industry headwinds as a result of US government-driven research spending cutbacks. The good news on Bio-Rad was that it repurchased shares at a strong pace in the first half of the year and avoided large, value-destructive M&A. The company also showed operational improvement and should be in a better position to grow FCF in 2026 and beyond. We added a partial position of Avantor to the portfolio in the second half of the year after (and then while) the company had some self-inflicted ups and downs. It is probable that the worst is in the past for Avantor after a leadership change and guidance reset, but we felt it best to exit and book a loss before year-end.

Exor (EXXRF) – European holding company Exor had a disappointing stock market performance in the quarter and year, with the share price correcting in 2025. This is despite a series of capital allocation and portfolio moves that fundamentally enhanced intrinsic value per share and continue to give us confidence in the leadership of CEO John Elkann. Exor executed a partial sale of Ferrari (RACE) early in the year at all-time highs, roughly 40% above year end levels, and redeployed the proceeds into Exor’s own shares at a sizable holding company discount, via an efficient one-time Dutch auction. Alongside that move, Exor increased its stake in Philips at good prices to roughly a 19% stake from 15% earlier in the year and supported the complex sale of CNH’s spinoff Iveco Group’s two separate businesses. Yet the market’s cooling toward Ferrari and a weak stock market year for both Stellantis and CNH (despite value per share at both not moving much) have weighed on Exor’s quoted price. With expectations around Ferrari reset after the company held its lukewarmly received November 2025 Capital Markets Day, and multiple self-help levers progressing across Exor’s other major holdings, we see 2026 offering us a compelling opportunity for the holding company discount to narrow meaningfully from today’s elevated levels.

Kraft Heinz (KHC) – Global food and beverage producer Kraft was a detractor for 2025. The market is overly focused on the lack of near term growth in North America and not focusing enough on the value-creating potential of the company's upcoming split into two businesses: the higher-growth Global Taste Elevation Co. which contains the Heinz brand and should garner a teens EBITDA multiple, and the stable remaining company comprised largely of North American grocery products, which can trade at the same multiple total Kraft currently trades today. This would result in a combined stock price over $40 per share. We are also extremely pleased with Steve Cahillane being named CEO. We got to know Steve during our successful investment in Kellanova / Kellogg’s, and we believe Steve is the perfect operator to lead Global Taste Elevation and help decide who should lead North American Grocery.

Portfolio Activity

The aforementioned Fund merger led to more activity in the Fund than usual this year and in the fourth quarter. At the end of the year, we owned certain positions that have already been exited as we write to you. We were also excited to “merge in” long-time International Fund holdings such as Gruma and Jollibee that we are now able to own in our Global strategy because there is more liquidity availability post-merger.

Additionally, we still own certain partial positions that we either started in the fourth quarter but have yet to finish or were also “merged-in” but are not yet to fuller-position status. We look forward to discussing all of this in more detail in our next letter but do not think it’s best to broadcast more at this moment.

Outlook

On the communications side of Southeastern, we have been pleased with the feedback on our Research Perspectives notes. Expect these to continue in 2026 and beyond. Our P/V Podcasts are not going away, but we only had one last year for a few company-specific reasons. Expect more (but continuing our focus on quality over quantity) of these going forward along with some external podcast appearances, one of which has already been recorded this month.

2025 had many positive signs for Southeastern. The best remains the February through April volatility around Liberation Day when we kept your money safe and materially outperformed the market. We want to stress that we are not simply building a portfolio for bad times only, but there will be virtue in all markets from owning real companies on offense in a time when excessive speculation is widespread. Understandable, durable, FCF generating assets run by great partners should win when the tide goes out and today’s fads like prediction markets, weird forms of crypto, and overhyped IPOs inevitably disappoint.

Thank you for your partnership. Here’s to the next 50 years.
